Congratulations. You've made it to the final course. This is when you finally hear the origination of the term black swan and why we named our company the black swan group. Until the 17th century in Europe, people could only imagine white swans because all swans seen up to that point had only been white. In London it became commonplace to refer to impossible things as black swans. Then in 1697 a Dutch explorer found himself on the western shore of Australia and spotted a black swan. Suddenly the unthinkable was reeled. Now seeing Nicholas Talib was inspired by this idea when he wrote one of the best business books of 2007, The Black Swan, The Impact of the Highly Improbable. Talib popularized the term black swan and used it to symbolize the tunnel vision nature of predictions based on previous experience. Black swan theory tells us that things happen we previously thought to be impossible or never even imagined at all. Now please don't confuse this with the cliche that sometimes things happen against one in a million odds, though in fact it wasn't that the odds were one in a million, but rather that the things we didn't see coming based on our experience do come to pass. Of course learning from the past is important, otherwise you repeat the same mistakes over and over again. At the same time please don't limit your expectations based on past experiences. You're smarter and more successful not just when you open yourself to the possibility of the unexpected but when you actively seek it out because you've learned it has to be there. We use black swans as a dual metaphor. First black swans are the hidden pieces of information unknown unknowns whose unearthing has game-changing effects. Those who know to actively look for black swans will experience negotiation breakthroughs that alter not just the course of their negotiations but their careers and subsequently their lives. Because of that we also use the black swan metaphor to describe how we negotiate, how you will negotiate when the two millimeter shifts in your negotiation approach have a massive impact on your outcomes. When you as a negotiator bring game-changing impacts to negotiations then you are a black swan. So if you engage in a phase of a negotiation and you haven't discovered anything new you should be worried that you failed yourself. In our description of unknown unknowns why are we doubling up on the term unknown? Isn't that a little redundant? We're highlighting that black swans are where the hidden overlap, where the hidden information which each side is holding back overlaps. This is the reality of dual unknowns. There is hidden information on both sides of the negotiation. Each side has closely held cards that the other side might only find after exhaustive research if at all, no matter what the amount of research. What happens when those hidden those dual sets of unknowns overlap? This is where the black swans are and there's no way of knowing their impact until you unearth them. The secondary impact and maybe even greater is that each side is in possession of information. They have no idea is important because they don't know the hidden cards of the other side. Black swans are crucial concept to exceptional negotiations. Each side during a negotiation is in possession of anywhere from three to five black swans. For you to take your negotiation prowess to the next level to become great, you've got to actively seek out the unknown unknowns. One key to this is retaining a beginner's mind. This is not relying on your experience but being open to the moment to actually enjoying the discovery. Things that don't add up should be reviewed not as meaningless anomalies but as clues. Read their nonverbal clues and voice those reads with your counterpart to unearth the black swans. Note the small pauses that suggest discomfort or withheld information and use simple labels like you seem hesitant or you seem uncomfortable. I guarantee you your counterpart has pieces of information whose value they don't understand. How important is what we don't know? What we don't know can kill deals. Here's the kicker. If you can accept that you don't know everything going in, that there are black swans to be uncovered, then you will realize that by definition there's a better deal to be had. Your goal, whatever it originally was, is not the best you can do. When you are open to the unknown possibilities, the black swans, you will gain momentum and you will drastically change the course of your negotiations. This course is designed to give you the tools of black swans.
